claim: Demographic divergence trade is the highest Sharpe ratio structural relative value opportunity
reason: Self-contradicting: 5/10 confidence, negative carry, 10-20 year uncertain timing, consensus for 5+ years without delivering. A trade with undefined holding period, negative carry, and 5/10 confidence cannot be described as highest Sharpe ratio. Japan falsifies simple 'bad demographics = bad assets' narrative. Directional thesis survives; trade characterization does not.

claim: Markets price at most 1-2 standard deviations of demographic shift into term premia
reason: No methodology provided for decomposing term premia into demographic vs non-demographic components. The r* confidence interval of 480bp swallows the claimed effect entirely. A weaker claim — current sovereign debt pricing may be inconsistent with long-run demographic fiscal arithmetic — is defensible.

claim: Private credit NAVs are overstated by 10-20% on average due to demographic-adjusted growth assumptions
reason: Applies a uniform growth assumption gap across a $2T asset class with enormous sector and borrower heterogeneity. Many borrowers in software and financial services face non-demographic constraints on growth. Directional claim survives; specific magnitude is unsubstantiated.

claim: GLP-1 agonists will produce fiscal savings of 1-2% of GDP annually
reason: Requires near-universal adoption, that drugs halve morbidity duration without extending lifespan with maintained morbidity, that drug costs don't offset savings, and that behavioral responses don't dilute the effect. None of these conditions is addressed. Directional possibility survives; quantified magnitude does not.

claim: Demographics are the single most important secular determinant of the long-end neutral rate
reason: Partial R-squared for demographics on r* is 0.15-0.30, meaning 70-85% of variance is explained by other factors. Claiming demographics is the single most important requires demonstrating it explains more variance than globalization, EM savings glut, or post-GFC deleveraging — which the evidence does not support.

claim: 3 out of 5 historical analogues show inflationary outcomes from aging
reason: Sample is cherry-picked and heterogeneous. US 1965-1980 was youth dependency plus oil shocks, not old-age dependency. UK 1950-1980 involved post-imperial adjustment and union power. Japan 2022-present is 3 years of data after 27 years of deflation. Restricted to pure old-age-dependency episodes, insufficient sample for a base rate.

claim: Japan's CLO market atrophy is a generalizable demographic template for other aging economies
reason: Japan's CLO atrophy was primarily driven by BoJ zero/negative interest rate policy and direct corporate bond purchases, which eliminated the spread justifying securitization. The US would need to replicate Japan's monetary policy configuration, not just demographics.

claim: Immigration reversal is invisible in futures markets until it shows up in grocery prices
reason: Agricultural futures already price labor expectations through basis differentials and regional spreads. H-2A visa expansion from 80K to 370K is market-observable. A sudden immigration reversal would register in specialty crop futures within weeks. The transmission channel is real; the invisibility claim is wrong.

claim: WWI Germany and WWII Japan serve as analogues for China's demographic-driven strategic adventurism
reason: Pre-WWI Germany was rising, not declining. Pre-WWII Japan's demographics bear little resemblance to modern China's. These analogies gesture at a general declining-power dynamic but the specific cases contradict the specific claim. The power transition framework is valid in the abstract; these examples weaken rather than strengthen it.

claim: Countries with median age below 25 have a 60-70% instability base rate
reason: Conflates correlation with causation. Countries with median ages below 25 are overwhelmingly low-income, post-colonial, and institutionally weak — the instability base rate for any low-income country over 20 years is extremely high regardless of demographics. Urdal (2006) finds youth bulges increase conflict probability by 30-40% conditional on poor governance, not 60-70% unconditionally.
